elements.sent-2411-002: This paper introduces the concept of autonomous microring arbitration, or wavelength arbitration, to address the challenge of multi-microring initialization in microring-based Dense-Wavelength-Division-Multiplexed (DWDM) transceivers.
elements.sent-2411-003: This arbitration is inherently policy-driven, defining critical system characteristics such as the spectral ordering of microrings.
elements.sent-2411-004: Furthermore, to facilitate large-scale deployment, the arbitration algorithms must operate independently of specific wavelength information and be resilient to system variability.
elements.sent-2411-005: Addressing these complexities requires a holistic approach that encompasses the entire system, from device-level variabilities to the transceiver interface—this system-wide perspective is the focus of this paper.
elements.sent-2411-006: To support efficient analysis, we develop a hierarchical framework incorporating an ideal, wavelength-aware arbitration model to examine arbitration failures at both the policy and algorithmic levels.
elements.sent-2411-007: The effectiveness of this approach is demonstrated in two ways: by analyzing the robustness of each policy in relation to device variabilities, and by developing an algorithm that achieves near-perfect alignment with the ideal model, offering superior robustness compared to the traditional sequential tuning method.
elements.sent-2411-008: The simulator code used in this paper is available at \url{https://github.com/wdmsim/wdm-simulator .}
elements.sent-2411-009: However, scaling up poses significant challenges in system design, particularly with regard to mass deployment scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-010: A cost-efficient, robust solution is essential, ideally equipped with a comprehensive analytical toolkit for large-scale deployment \cite{sun2012dsent, lee2022beyond, nedovic2023methodology .}
elements.sent-2411-011: For microring-based DWDM transceivers, one major challenge is aligning microring resonances with laser wavelengths—a problem of initialization \cite{mak2017multivariable —which requires judicious arbitration of the microrings to be cost-efficient, robust and autonomous.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Microring resonance control, a common technique for addressing microring initialization \cite{sun201645, li2014silicon, de2019power, padmaraju2013wavelength, grimaldi2022self, li20203 , involves sweeping the microring resonance across the tuning range and identifying the resonance alignment.}
elements.sent-2411-013: For a single microring with a laser tone, the resonance alignment is identified by the peak in intra-cavity optical power (wavelength search) \cite{sun201645, li2014silicon, de2019power , followed by a feedback loop that stabilize the resonance wavelength (wavelength lock) \cite{padmaraju2013wavelength, grimaldi2022self, li20203 .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: However, in DWDM systems, the wavelength search would yield multiple peaks due to the multi-wavelength laser, necessitating an “informed” decision to guarantee maximum wavelength allocation \cite{hattink2020automated ; we will call this wavelength arbitration.}
elements.sent-2411-015: To achieve a scalable arbitration system, we introduce a hierarchical framework for wavelength arbitration.
elements.sent-2411-016: In our framework, arbitration is fundamentally policy-driven, with policies defining the wavelength interface of the DWDM transceiver.
elements.sent-2411-017: The implementing algorithm and supporting circuit-level architecture then operate according to these policies.
elements.sent-2411-018: The primary function of arbitration, therefore, is to effectively coordinate microrings while accounting for device-level variations and system-wide constraints, ensuring both robustness and autonomy.
elements.sent-2411-019: While this holistic view is desirable, the challenge lies in efficiently integrating these policies with scalable algorithms and architectures to maintain optimal system performance under varying conditions.
elements.sent-2411-020: To address this, we introduce an arbitration model to decouple the analysis of policy and algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-021: This model has two components: an ideal, wavelength-aware model and a wavelength-oblivious model.
elements.sent-2411-022: The ideal model evaluates the arbitration policy under the assumption of wavelength-awareness, ensuring policies are effective and well-defined.
elements.sent-2411-023: In contrast, the wavelength-oblivious model evaluates the algorithm under practical conditions, where conflict resolution and wavelength allocation are performed without specific wavelength information.
elements.sent-2411-024: This approach allows us to evaluate arbitration robustness by analyzing failure probabilities across policies and algorithms, while also developing an algorithm that aligns closely with the ideal model, achieving scalability and robustness for high-volume deployment.
elements.sent-2411-025: This paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-026: We begin with an overview of the system model in \mysecref{model and provide a formal definition of robustness metrics in \mysecref{metric .}}
elements.section-2411-002: Generalized Wavelength-Domain Model for Wavelength Arbitration
elements.sent-2411-027: Lock-to-Deterministic (LtD) allows only a single spectral ordering.
elements.sent-2411-028: Lock-to-Cyclic (LtC) allows any cyclic equivalent of the specified ordering.
elements.sent-2411-029: Lock-to-Any (LtA) does not impose any restrictions on the final spectral ordering.
elements.sent-2411-030: These enforcement levels have an inclusive relationship, with LtA being the most permissive and LtD being the most restrictive.
elements.sent-2411-031: MWL stands for Multi-Wavelength Laser, and MRR stands for Microring Resonator.
elements.sent-2411-032: We assume a typical DWDM transceiver architecture \cite{liang2022energy where a multi-wavelength laser propagates into a shared waveguide bus and to downstream microring resonators.}
elements.sent-2411-033: During transceiver initialization, each microring captures one of the light wavelengths within its tuning range.
elements.sent-2411-034: This paper focuses on microring wavelength management at initialization, which is represented by the wavelength arbiter in \myfigref{fig_2 (a).}
elements.sent-2411-035: For simplicity, the wavelength initialization process is projected onto the wavelength space; thus, only the wavelength domain is considered throughout the paper.
elements.sent-2411-036: In a typical microring-based DWDM transceiver, the local wavelength status is tracked and updated by the per-microring tuner and sensor circuit \cite{lee2022beyond, levy20248, xuan2023256 .}
elements.sent-2411-037: We define the wavelength arbiter as an entity responsible for global wavelength management, maintaining the global wavelength status and overseeing the coordination of the microrings.
elements.sent-2411-038: The outcome of wavelength arbitration is determined by policy, while we define algorithm as the detailed steps to achieve this outcome.
elements.sent-2411-039: For clarity, the algorithm discussed in this paper is wavelength-oblivious; it can access and update the local wavelength status only through the per-microring tuner and sensor.
elements.sent-2411-040: To explain policy, we first describe the role of microring resonance wavelength in PHY-level data transmission.
elements.sent-2411-041: Just as a port serves a transmission point in the spatial domain, a microring acts as a port in the wavelength-domain.
elements.sent-2411-042: For instance, assume an electrical transmit lane drives a microring at wavelength $\lambda_{1}$, and on the receive side, another microring at resonance wavelength $\lambda_{1}$ is connected to an electrical receive lane.
elements.sent-2411-043: When the host sends packet $\text{P 1$ through the electrical transmit lane, it is retransmitted optically at wavelength $\lambda_{1}$, and received by the receive microring, reaching the client side.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Thus, optical connectivity for $\text{P 1$ is determined by wavelength $\lambda_{1}$, serving as a virtual optical lane for data transmission.}
elements.sent-2411-045: In the context of DWDM, microring spectral ordering can be understood as optical lane ordering (or equivalently, optical port mapping).
elements.sent-2411-046: From a communication standpoint, the transmitting and receiving endpoints should agree on the data ordering.
elements.sent-2411-047: In microring-based DWDM systems, it is preferred that they are cognizant of optical lane ordering during electrical-to-optical and optical-to-electrical conversions\footnote{One may argue that bit orderings at both endpoints are handled at MAC-level serialization and deserialization.}
elements.sent-2411-048: However, from a transceiver perspective, it is useful to keep track of on-the-fly lane ordering, especially for low-latency applications \cite{sharma2023system .. .}
elements.sent-2411-049: We assume an optical port remapper (bit-shuffler backend in Georgas et al.\ \cite{georgas2011addressing ) at the optical transceiver backend to ensure the electrical interfaces at both ends see identical ordering and streamline the data communication.}
elements.sent-2411-050: The level of remapping, proportional to the power and latency cost of the backend, is determined by the enforcement level over the microring spectral ordering post-arbitration.
elements.sent-2411-051: In this paper, we adopt the enforcement level as an arbitration policy.
elements.sent-2411-052: As shown in \myfigref{fig_2 (b), we consider three wavelength arbitration policies: Lock-to-Deterministic (LtD), Lock-to-Cyclic (LtC) and Lock-to-Any (LtA), each corresponding to strong, medium and weak ordering enforcement, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-053: LtD policy demonstrates the strongest enforcement on the post-arbitration microring spectral ordering.
elements.sent-2411-054: For example, if the target microring spectral ordering $\left(0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5\right)$ is specified, the arbiter pairs microrings with laser wavelengths in that exact order and disallows any other spectral orderings.
elements.sent-2411-055: By assigning microrings to a predetermined spectral ordering, it can effectively preserve optical lane ordering, bypassing any issues of optical lane shuffling.
elements.sent-2411-056: Spectral-domain link optimization techniques can be applied more easily, such as inter-microring crosstalk minimization \cite{bahadori2016crosstalk .}
elements.sent-2411-057: However, despite its apparent advantages, it is challenging to implement due to high microring tuning range requirements, as we will show in \mysecref{analysis .}
elements.sent-2411-058: LtC policy relaxes the enforcement by allowing cyclic equivalents of the specified target spectral ordering.
elements.sent-2411-059: For example, if the target microring spectral ordering $\left(0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5\right)$ is specified as in \myfigref{fig_2 , the arbiter can assign microring wavelengths in cyclic equivalents of the ordering, such as $\left(2, 3, 4, 5, 0, 1\right)$, $\left(0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5\right)$.}
elements.sent-2411-060: The required microring tuning range is smaller than that of the LtD policy.
elements.sent-2411-061: This is because a linear shift in spectral ordering effectively cancels any global offsets in laser and microring wavelengths due to ring resonance periodicity\footnote{Assume R0, R1, R2, R3 with \qty{4 {\nano\metre FSR are assigned to \qtylist{1300;1301;1302;1303 {\nano\metre laser wavelengths in the $\left(0,1,2,3\right)$ order.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: Due to resonance periodicity by FSR, microrings assigned to \qtylist{1301;1302;1303;1304 {\nano\metre (shift-by-1 order) effectively shift wavelengths by \qty{1 {\nano\metre .. \cite{georgas2011addressing, krishnamoorthy2011exploiting .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: Optical port remapping can also be done with smaller overhead using a configurable barrel-shifter in hardware \cite{georgas2011addressing or byte-shifting in software.}
elements.sent-2411-064: LtA policy does not impose any restrictions on the final microring spectral ordering.
elements.sent-2411-065: In other words, it allows any spectral ordering post-arbitration, such as $\left(2, 0, 1, 4, 5, 3\right)$, $\left(2, 3, 4, 5, 0, 1\right)$, or $\left(0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5\right)$.
elements.sent-2411-066: Intuitively, it has the least requirement on microring tuning range and is most amenable to tuning power optimization techniques \cite{wang2020energy, wu2018pairing .}
elements.sent-2411-067: However, the resulting spectral ordering is random; optical port remapping should be done by a configurable crossbar (bit-reorder mux in Georgas et al.\ \cite{georgas2011addressing ) or byte-reordering in software, potentially incurring overhead to the system.}

elements.sent-2411-120: In this section, we introduce the evaluation methodology for arbitration robustness.
elements.sent-2411-121: Previously, tuning strategies have been evaluated based on their resulting tuning power, which facilitates comparisons of algorithm efficiency \cite{georgas2011addressing, wang2020energy, wang2018energy, wu2018pairing .}
elements.sent-2411-122: However, tuning power assumes arbitration success, whereas evaluating robustness requires accounting for arbitration failures.
elements.sent-2411-123: Moreover, policy and algorithm represent distinct aspects of arbitration design—system specification and implementation—and their separation must be accounted for in an efficient, hierarchical analysis.
elements.sent-2411-124: This necessitates a more detailed approach to devising simulation experiments and assessing arbitration robustness.
elements.sent-2411-125: The experiments sample multi-wavelength lasers (MWL) and microring resonator (MRR) rows, subjecting them to arbitration tests under different policies.
elements.sent-2411-126: Policy-level evaluation uses the ideal arbitration model, which assumes wavelength-awareness and calculates AFP based on failure statistics.
elements.sent-2411-127: Algorithm-level evaluation, on the other hand, employs a wavelength-oblivious arbitration model to reflect operational constraints, computing CAFP to assess how closely the algorithm approximates ideal arbitration success.
elements.sent-2411-128: In particular, different policies correspond to varying levels of spectral ordering enforcement, leading to distinct sets of device parameters for arbitration success, which can be deduced from the wavelength-aware arbitration experiments.
elements.sent-2411-129: We define Arbitration Failure Probability (AFP) as a metric to assess arbitration robustness under specific policy and varying system configurations.
elements.sent-2411-130: The system-under-test is generated by sampling multi-wavelength lasers and microring rows, with variations determined by their respective sources.
elements.sent-2411-131: Each system-under-test is subjected to an arbitration test using a specific policy and the ideal arbitration model.
elements.sent-2411-132: The number of arbitration failures is recorded, and AFP is calculated by dividing this number by the total number of trials.
elements.sent-2411-133: Essentially, AFP reflects the arbitration yield, where failure to arbitrate successfully is treated as transceiver failure, assuming accurate device models.
elements.sent-2411-134: Since the algorithm operates in a wavelength-oblivious manner, the simulation setup uses the wavelength-oblivious arbitration model to reflect the algorithm's operational constraints.
elements.sent-2411-135: While AFP can be used for algorithm evaluation, it may obscure the effectiveness of the algorithm due to the influence of the policy it implements.
elements.sent-2411-136: To isolate the effect of the algorithm implementation, we introduce a new metric: Conditional Arbitration Failure Probability (CAFP).
elements.sent-2411-137: CAFP is defined as the conditional failure probability of the arbitration algorithm, given that the ideal arbitration under the same policy is successful.
elements.sent-2411-138: This metric quantifies how closely the algorithm approximates the behavior of the ideal arbitration model.
elements.sent-2411-139: CAFP is calculated by running tests using a specific algorithm and the wavelength-oblivious model, and recording failures that occur when the same system-under-test, under the corresponding policy, achieves arbitration success in the ideal model.
elements.sent-2411-140: The total failure rate, considering both the algorithm and the policy, can also be computed using conditional probability as follows: \begin{IEEEeqnarray {rCl P_{\text{alg (\text{fail ) = P_{\text{alg |\text{succ (\text{fail ) P(\text{succ ) + P_{\text{alg |\text{fail (\text{fail ) P(\text{fail ) \nonumber = P_{\text{alg |\text{succ (\text{fail ) P(\text{succ ) + P(\text{fail ) \end{IEEEeqnarray where, according to our notations, $P_{\text{alg (\text{fail )$ denotes the total failure probability, $P_{\text{alg |\text{succ (\text{fail )$ represents the CAFP, $P(\text{fail )$ is the AFP of the corresponding policy, and $P(\text{succ ) = 1 - P(\text{fail )$ is the probability of policy success.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-141: Note that $P_{\text{alg |\text{fail (\text{fail ) = 1$, as the algorithm will always fail when the ideal arbitration fails.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-142: In this section, we compare different arbitration policies and analyze their tradeoffs.
elements.sent-2411-143: We assume that three key parameters are considered during the arbitration design phase: arbitration policy, pre-fabrication microring spectral ordering $\left(r_{i}\right)$ and target post-arbitration microring spectral ordering $\left(s_{i}\right)$.
elements.sent-2411-144: We select four main test cases for policy evaluation, namely LtA-N/A, LtA-P/A, LtC-N/N, and LtC-P/P, which are summarized in \mytabref{arb_cfg .}
elements.sent-2411-145: LtA and LtC stand for Lock-to-Any and Lock-to-Cyclic policies, as defined in \mysecref{model-arbiter , according to their levels of spectral ordering enforcement.}
elements.sent-2411-146: For pre-fabrication spectral ordering, we consider two specific instances: Natural and Permuted ordering.
elements.sent-2411-147: Natural refers to a straightforward ordering of $\left(0, 1, 2, \cdots, N_{ch -1\right)$ for $N_{ch}$-DWDM.}

elements.sent-2411-208: RelSearch stands for microring relation search.
elements.sent-2411-209: Search Table (ST) entries record the tuner codes corresponding to the peak intra-microring optical power observed during the wavelength sweep (wavelength values are shown for illustration purposes).
elements.sent-2411-210: We focus on the LtC policy implementation, while extensions to other policies are discussed in \mysecref{scheme-discussion .}
elements.sent-2411-211: Our work extends the autonomous algorithm explained in Hattink et al.\ \cite{hattink2020automated by generalizing concepts such as target spectral ordering, conflict resolution, and maximum matching.}
elements.sent-2411-212: The algorithm consists of two phases: record and matching, as illustrated in \myfigref{fig_10 (a).}
elements.sent-2411-213: The record phase tabulates the wavelength-domain relationship between the microring wavelength search outcomes of microring pairs.
elements.sent-2411-214: It is followed by the matching phase, where each microring is assigned to different laser wavelengths according to the identified relationships and the specified microring spectral ordering.
elements.sent-2411-215: For convenience, we refer to the relationship between microring wavelength search outcomes as the microring relation, and the process of identifying this relationship as the relation search.
elements.sent-2411-216: During the record phase, $N_{ch}$ (4 in \myfigref{fig_10 (b)) relation searches are conducted to discover the microring relations between consecutive microring pairs.}
elements.sent-2411-217: These pairs are derived from the target spectral ordering $s_{i}$.
elements.sent-2411-218: In \myfigref{fig_10 (b), given $s_{i =\left(0,1,2,3\right)$, the target pairs are $\left(0,1\right)$, $\left(1,2\right)$, $\left(2,3\right)$, and $\left(3,0\right)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-220: We note that relation searches are not run across all $N_{ch}\times(N_{ch -1)/2$ microring pairs to construct a comprehensive “relation map” of wavelength search outcomes.}
elements.sent-2411-221: Instead, we run relation searches on $N_{ch}$ microring pairs.
elements.sent-2411-222: This is because the wavelength allocation for the LtC policy is semi-deterministic; if consecutive microring pairs from $s_{i}$ are allocated to neighboring wavelengths, then the wavelengths are assigned as desired.
elements.sent-2411-223: For example, it is sufficient for R$\left(i+1\right)$ to be assigned to the next laser wavelength of that assigned to R$i$ when $s_{i =\left(0,1,2,3\right)$.}
elements.sent-2411-224: This property allows us to simplify the global arbitration problem into a set of arbitrations between consecutive pairs while guaranteeing robust operation.
elements.sent-2411-225: Leveraging this simplification, the matching phase performs a non-iterative microring-to-laser matching by building the global lock allocation table.
elements.sent-2411-226: The table is constructed by rearranging the microring search tables based on the relations between consecutive pairs established during the record phase.
elements.sent-2411-227: From this table, the final wavelength allocation is derived in a single pass, with each microring assigned its lock target from its respective search table.
elements.sent-2411-228: The full procedure is detailed in \mysecref{scheme-match .}
elements.sent-2411-229: Before we delve into each phase, we define the nomenclature that are used in the rest of the sections.
elements.sent-2411-230: The search table in \myfigref{fig_10 (b) is the recorded tuner codes of the microring corresponding to peak intra-cavity optical power during wavelength search.}
elements.sent-2411-231: We denote the search table of the $i$th microring as ST$\left(i\right)$.
elements.sent-2411-232: A microring relation connects the two search tables and is mathematically formulated as the relation index, representing the offset between the search table entry locations according to the wavelength correspondence.
elements.sent-2411-233: We denote the relation index between the $i$th and the $j$th microring as $RI\left(i, j\right)$.
elements.sent-2411-234: We use the relation index to align the search tables by wavelength alignment, forming the lock allocation table for efficient arbitration.
elements.sent-2411-235: The outcome of the relation search is a relation map between Search Tables, represented by Relation Index (RI).
elements.sent-2411-236: Blue region: aggressor microring tuning range.
elements.sent-2411-237: Green region: victim microring tuning range.
elements.sent-2411-238: The procedure is described in Hattink et al.\ \cite{hattink2020automated which utilizes the natural precedence order in light receive between the microrings.}
elements.sent-2411-239: In a typical microring-based DWDM architecture, when light propagates to the downstream microrings, a microring physically closer to the light input has priority in light receive over farther rings.
elements.sent-2411-240: In other words, physical prior maps to the precedence order in capturing the wavelengths.
elements.sent-2411-241: Using this property, the relation search assigns the aggressor and victim roles within the microring pair according to their physical order.
elements.sent-2411-242: Both microrings first undergo the wavelength search, recording their initial search tables.
elements.sent-2411-243: Then, the aggressor microring “injects” aggression into the victim microring by wavelength lock to a specific target from ST$\left(i\right)$.
elements.sent-2411-244: If the target is set appropriately, the victim microring will find one of the entries in its search table masked, indicating that the target chosen from ST$\left(i\right)$ and the masked entry in ST$\left(j\right)$ correspond to the same wavelength.
elements.sent-2411-245: Furthermore, other relations between the search table pairs can be inferred.
elements.sent-2411-246: For example, in \myfigref{fig_11 , if 3rd entry of ST$\left(i\right)$ and 2nd entry of ST$\left(j\right)$ correspond to the same wavelength, then 2nd entry of ST$\left(i\right)$ and 1st entry of ST$\left(j\right)$ also correspond to the same wavelength, given that the microring tuning is monotonic in wavelength-domain.}
elements.sent-2411-247: We call these identified and inferred relations a relation map between the microrings, and define Relation Index as the difference in indices between the masked entry and the aggressor target entry from each search table.
elements.sent-2411-248: Note that the aggressor target wavelength should be within the victim microring tuning range, which is not trivial in a wavelength-oblivious scenario.
elements.sent-2411-249: To achieve a proper aggressor injection, it is most straightforward to loop through the aggressor search table entries and repeat the relation search.

elements.sent-2411-324: In this paper, we addressed the critical challenge of aligning microring resonances with laser wavelengths in microring-based DWDM transceivers, a process we termed wavelength arbitration.
elements.sent-2411-325: We introduced an ideal arbitration model that decouples the analysis of policy and algorithm, providing a framework for evaluating the efficacy of different arbitration strategies.
elements.sent-2411-326: Our hierarchical formulation allows for the independent assessment of arbitration policies from algorithm implementations, simplifying the analysis of both layers.
elements.sent-2411-327: To effectively define the DWDM interface, we introduced three arbitration policies—LtA, LtC, and LtD—categorized by their spectral ordering enforcement levels and evaluated their tradeoffs in the design space.
elements.sent-2411-328: Furthermore, we developed a wavelength-oblivious algorithm for LtC policy implementation, including the RS/SSM and VT-RS/SSM.
elements.sent-2411-329: Through extensive simulations, we demonstrated that our schemes closely approximate the ideal model, significantly outperforming the traditional sequential tuning approach.
elements.sent-2411-330: Our findings underscore the importance of adopting a holistic approach to wavelength arbitration, where policy-driven arbitration, alongside algorithmic implementations, enables scalable and reliable wavelength allocation.
elements.sent-2411-331: By evaluating arbitration strategies through AFP and CAFP metrics, we provide a comprehensive view of how these strategies perform across varying device-level conditions and system configurations.
elements.sent-2411-332: This work establishes a foundation for advanced arbitration techniques, demonstrating the need to integrate both high-level policy design and practical, wavelength-oblivious algorithm implementation.
elements.sent-2411-333: Moving forward, further research into variations such as LtA policy implementations will be crucial for enhancing the scalability and robustness of microring-based DWDM transceivers.

elements.sent-2411-526: This subsection discusses the broader implications of the proposed arbitration algorithms, including considerations for physical implementation and potential directions for future exploration.
elements.sent-2411-527: Physical Implementation Aspect: This aspect can be viewed as a practical complement to the algorithm discussions.

elements.sent-2411-531: One of the practical challenges with the physical implementation is managing thermal crosstalk during arbitration runtime, especially in densely integrated photonic circuits.
elements.sent-2411-532: While arbitration primarily focuses on wavelength allocation or coarse wavelength alignment—which is inherently less sensitive to thermal effects than fine alignment—thermal variability can still impact the reliability of these operations.
elements.sent-2411-533: Addressing these challenges is crucial for robust operation at scale, and techniques such as Thermal Eigenmode Decomposition \cite{milanizadeh2019canceling could help mitigate these effects during runtime.}

elements.sent-2411-535: Future Outlooks: We outline potential directions for future work.
elements.sent-2411-536: First, the algorithm implementations of the LtD and LtA policies are left for future exploration.
elements.sent-2411-537: For the LtD policy, a pilot signal is likely necessary to determine the absolute ordering of microring resonances.
elements.sent-2411-538: If the algorithm relies on wavelength search outcomes, as in our proposed approach, some microrings may detect laser wavelengths from different FSRs during the search, thus limiting the ordering to modulo FSR.
elements.sent-2411-539: For the LtA policy, we note that it has the most relaxed requirement on microring spectral ordering, providing designers with the opportunity to explore algorithms such as tuning power minimization.
elements.sent-2411-540: Second, the framework can be extended to account for transceiver-level interactions, defining joint allocation policies for both transmitter and receiver microrings.
